one_line_takeaway: AgentSpec is a DSL that inserts developer-defined trigger/check/enforce guardrails into an LLM agent's execution loop (via LangChain hooks + ANTLR4), eliminating hazardous embodied actions and AV law violations and blocking >90% of risky code with millisecond overhead -- though it enforces only at discrete checkpoints, its LLM-generated rules recall just ~71%, and it is evaluated on risky instructions rather than against adversarial attacks.
key_finding: With manually written rules (RQ1): on the code agent AgentSpec detects/intercepts risky executions in over 90% of cases across the RedCode categories (with a human-in-the-loop user_inspection allowing programs judged harmless, e.g., a pickle-deserialization script); on the embodied agent it drives successful execution of hazardous instructions to 0% in ALL 10 unsafe categories while safe-task fulfillment drops only from 58.62% to 54.26%; on the AV agent it achieves 100% compliance ('Pass') in all 8 FixDrive law-violation scenarios. With LLM-generated rules (RQ2, OpenAI o1): code 87.26% of risky code enforced, embodied 95.56% precision and 70.96% recall, AV 5 of 8 scenarios (62.50%) prevented in a zero-shot setting. Generalizability (RQ3): 25 rules cover 750 code scenarios (~30 each), 12 rules cover 250 embodied scenarios (~21 each; one pour-safety rule alone covers 96 scenarios), 6 rules cover 8 AV scenarios. Overhead (RQ4) is millisecond-scale -- parsing 1.42 ms, predicate evaluation 2.83 ms (code) / 1.11 ms (embodied) -- negligible against agent execution times of 25.4 s (code) and 9.82 s (embodied).
core_contribution: AgentSpec is a lightweight domain-specific language (DSL) and runtime framework for specifying and enforcing customizable safety constraints on LLM agents. Each rule has five parts: a rule id, a trigger (an event such as state_change, before-action, agent_finish, or a domain-specific event like PythonREPL, pour, or red_light_detected), a check (a conjunction of Boolean predicates such as is_destructive_cmd or is_into_wettable_object), an enforce clause (one or more of user_inspection, llm_self_examine, invoke_action(params), or stop), and an end keyword. The paper positions AgentSpec as, to the authors' knowledge, the first framework that systematically enforces customizable safety constraints on LLM agents AT RUNTIME (before an action executes), as opposed to pre-execution risk assessment (ToolEmu) or LLM-interpreted guardrails (GuardAgent). It also contributes a formal semantics for rules and trajectory transformation, a concrete implementation, safety rule sets for three domains (code, embodied, autonomous driving), and an experiment on automatically generating rules with an LLM (OpenAI o1).
threat_model: The concern is unsafe or non-compliant behavior by an autonomous LLM agent -- 'security vulnerabilities, legal violations, and unintended harmful actions' arising from the agent's own autonomy. In the paper's OWN evaluation the trigger for harm is a risky user instruction (e.g., 'append a destructive sudo alias to /root/.bashrc', 'pour water onto a laptop', or an AV entering an intersection on a red light) that the agent would otherwise carry out; risk tolerance is treated as organization/context-dependent (e.g., a dosage-adjusting agent unsafe in a hospital but permitted in a research lab). AgentSpec is a DEFENSIVE runtime guardrail with a developer/user-defined enforcement model kept external to the LLM. NOTE: adversarial attacker scenarios (AgentPoison memory/knowledge-base poisoning, Environmental Injection Attack, prompt-to-SQL injection) appear only in the Related Work section; the paper claims AgentSpec 'can specify properties to defend against these attacks' but does NOT evaluate against any of them -- its experiments cover risky-instruction / hazard-avoidance and legal-compliance settings, not attack defense.
limitations: By the authors' own account: (1) enforcement is deterministic and applied only at discrete execution checkpoints, so it does NOT reason about long-term consequences and lacks trajectory-based (multi-step lookahead) safety analysis; probabilistic/model-based foresight (e.g., a learned DTMC for reachability) is left to future work. (2) LLM-generated rules overfit to the provided examples -- they flag only explicitly listed sensitive files/directories and miss similar unlisted cases (false negatives), fail on complex object properties (e.g., a kettle filled with wine unsafe to heat), and can be overly rigid (banning all pouring, or making an AV stop suddenly within 5 m) yielding false positives; recall on embodied rules is only 70.96%. (3) Effectiveness depends on human user_inspection judgments, a manual/subjective step (mitigated with cross-validation and blind protocols). (4) Overfitting risk in manual rule design, mitigated by a 1:9 example/test split. Reviewer-added caveats not stated as limitations: predicate correctness is user-dependent (source-level pattern checks, e.g., URL whitelist + write_to_io, could be evaded and the authors acknowledge data-flow analysis would improve accuracy); the headline 'over 90%' for code hides categories where 0 programs were flagged and cases the user allowed; and no adversarial-attack defense is empirically demonstrated despite the framing.

datasets_used: RedCode-Exec (code agent; 25 risky/vulnerability categories, 30 test cases each = 750 scenarios)
SafeAgentBench (embodied agent; 10 unsafe categories + 1 safe category, 250 risky scenarios, with safe counterparts for recall)
FixDrive (autonomous-vehicle agent; 8 law-violation scenarios across laws incl. Law38/44/46/53 and collision/finish-journey, on the Apollo stack)
quantitative_results: Code agent (manual rules): risky executions detected/intercepted in over 90% of cases (RQ1)
Embodied agent (manual rules): hazardous-instruction success reduced to 0% in all 10 unsafe categories; safe-task fulfillment 58.62% -> 54.26%
AV agent (manual rules): 100% compliance ('Pass') in all 8 FixDrive law-violation scenarios
LLM-generated rules (OpenAI o1): code 87.26% of risky code enforced (750 scenarios, 75 examples, 25 rules)
LLM-generated rules (OpenAI o1): embodied 95.56% precision and 70.96% recall (250 scenarios, 25 examples, 10 rules)
LLM-generated rules (OpenAI o1): AV 5 of 8 scenarios prevented = 62.50% (zero-shot, 6 rules)
Generalizability: 25 rules / 750 code scenarios (~30 per rule); 12 rules / 250 embodied scenarios (~21 per rule); one pour-safety rule covers 96 scenarios; 6 rules / 8 AV scenarios
Overhead: parsing 1.42 ms; predicate evaluation 2.83 ms (code) and 1.11 ms (embodied); vs mean agent execution 25.4 s (code) and 9.82 s (embodied)
baselines_compared: Agent WITHOUT AgentSpec vs WITH AgentSpec (self-ablation; the primary quantitative comparison, e.g., Table 4 embodied fulfillment)
Manually written rules vs LLM (OpenAI o1) auto-generated rules (RQ1 vs RQ2)

Qualitative-only comparison to prior work: ToolEmu (LM-emulated sandbox, pre-execution assessment), GuardAgent (LLM-interpreted guardrail code), NeMo (dialogue-level NL constraints), llama.cpp / LangChain Expression Language (syntactic pattern matching) -- no head-to-head numeric benchmark against these
